text: This demonstration will be of the laparoscopic slip knot. So the suture is loaded the usual way.
The surgeon takes a bite with the suture needle, preparing to tie a slip knot. The first throw is made, leaving the initial knot loose like an air knot.
You're going to do your next throw. You want to tighten this.

The surgeon manipulates the suture horizontally into an air knot, pulling the needle-end towards the five o'clock position on the screen to create tension. The opposite leg of the suture is pushed towards the Penrose drain, resulting in the knot flipping visibly.
The surgeon maintains tension with the right hand while sliding along the suture, using a pulling motion with the right hand to snug down the knot into a slip knot configuration.
The surgeon squares the knot by bringing the body and tail of the suture together and tightening them horizontally, ensuring the knot is secure.
Now that should actually hold your tension very nicely so that you can place additional throws. without losing your tension note. It's a very useful technique for closing the diaphragmatic cura.
